name: Texas Dept of Public Safety (DPS)
tagline: Texas' statewide law-enforcement and homeland-security agency — Texas Rangers, Highway Patrol, driver license, border security, and the state fusion center — running a large field, mobile, data-center, and biometrics technology footprint.
overview: The Texas Department of Public Safety (DPS) is the state's primary law-enforcement and homeland-security agency. It runs the Texas Rangers, the Texas Highway Patrol, the statewide driver license and ID program, crime records and forensics, border-security operations, and the Texas Fusion Center — the 24/7 intelligence hub that serves as the state repository for homeland-security information. With roughly 11,000 commissioned and non-commissioned personnel, DPS combines a heavy mobile and field-operations profile (patrol vehicles, in-car and body-worn systems, mobile data) with substantial back-office data-center, records, and biometrics systems. Its technology estate spans the Multimodal Biometric Identification System (MBIS) for fingerprint, mugshot, and iris matching; the driver license issuance and online-services platforms (including the MyTexas app); CJIS-governed criminal-justice data; and real-time intelligence/analytics for the fusion center. For a Dell SLG seller, DPS is a high-value account that touches edge/mobile, secure data center, storage, biometrics, AI/analytics, and cybersecurity — most of it procurable through DIR Cooperative Contracts and gated by TX-RAMP and CJIS requirements.

budget.intro: DPS is a large direct spender. The FY2026–27 biennial budget (SB 1, 89th Legislature) funds DPS at roughly $3.8B, with an IT capital line of about $134M and record continued border-security funding driving much of the field, surveillance, and intelligence spend.

stats.label: Border security (state)
stats.note: FY26–27; Operation Lone Star, surveillance, wall — much flows through DPS

notes: Border-security appropriations fund surveillance, intelligence, and field technology — a large recurring driver of DPS tech demand.
The 89th Legislature created a new Homeland Security Division within DPS, reshaping where border and intelligence programs sit.
Statewide cybersecurity functions are moving from DIR to the new Texas Cyber Command (transition through Dec 31, 2026); DPS retains its own operational cyber and CJIS-security responsibilities.

initiatives.name: MBIS biometrics modernization (cloud)
initiatives.summary: DPS's Multimodal Biometric Identification System matches fingerprints, mugshots, and irises for arrest and applicant submissions. DPS has solicited cloud-based maintenance, support, disaster recovery, and enhancement services — a clear infrastructure, storage, and resiliency opening tied to a mission-critical system.

initiatives.name: Driver license modernization & digital services
initiatives.summary: DPS rolled out a new, harder-to-counterfeit DL/ID card design (Aug 2025) and expanded online application, renewal, and replacement via the MyTexas app. Texas has not yet launched a mobile/digital driver license, an area of active national expansion and a likely future modernization track.

initiatives.name: Texas Fusion Center & real-time intelligence
initiatives.summary: The fusion center's Real-Time Watch Center operates 24/7 as the state's homeland-security information hub, consolidating threat and suspicious-activity data across federal, state, and local partners. Real-time data ingestion, analytics, and AI for threat detection map directly to high-performance compute, storage, and edge needs.

initiatives.name: Border-security field & surveillance technology
initiatives.summary: Record border funding sustains surveillance infrastructure, mobile and edge compute, ruggedized field devices, and intelligence operations under Operation Lone Star and the new Homeland Security Division — a sustained driver of edge, mobile, and data-center demand.

procurement.intro: DPS buys both through statewide vehicles and via its own agency solicitations. The most efficient path for IT and infrastructure is DIR Cooperative Contracts; larger or specialized systems (e.g., MBIS, driver license platforms) run as agency RFOs/RFPs. Any cloud or as-a-service offering must clear TX-RAMP, and criminal-justice data carries CJIS security obligations.

vehicles.name: DIR Cooperative Contracts
vehicles.detail: Primary statewide vehicle — pre-competed contracts DPS can buy hardware, software, cloud, and services through without a standalone RFP. Confirm Dell's coverage and scope.

vehicles.name: Agency RFOs / RFPs
vehicles.detail: Major or specialized systems (MBIS biometrics, driver license platforms, surveillance) run as DPS-issued Requests for Offers/Proposals — track DPS and Texas SmartBuy/ESBD postings.

vehicles.name: TX-RAMP certification
vehicles.detail: Mandatory cloud-security gate: cloud / as-a-service offerings must hold the right TX-RAMP level (NIST 800-53 Low/Moderate) to be sellable. Clear early.

vehicles.name: CJIS compliance
vehicles.detail: Criminal-justice information systems must meet FBI CJIS Security Policy requirements — a hard constraint on any solution touching DPS records or biometrics data.

powerStructure.name: Col. Freeman F. Martin
powerStructure.title: Director, Texas DPS
powerStructure.note: Sworn in Dec 1, 2024 — the first Texas Ranger to lead DPS; previously Senior Deputy Director over Homeland Security. Sets agency direction; the top relationship for the account.

powerStructure.name: Jessica Ballew
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer
powerStructure.note: CIO since Sept 2024, leading technology strategy with a stated focus on innovation, interoperability, and IT modernization. The primary IT decision-maker and key relationship.

powerStructure.name: Operations, Solutions & Delivery (OSD)
powerStructure.title: IT & data infrastructure service area
powerStructure.note: Manages enterprise IT and data infrastructure, statewide interoperability, the Innovation Office, Transformation (PMO), and the Enterprise Data Office — the buying center for infrastructure and modernization.

powerStructure.name: DPS Cyber Security
powerStructure.title: Agency cybersecurity & CJIS security
powerStructure.note: Owns DPS network defense, risk management, and CJIS-security obligations. Note statewide cyber policy is moving to the Texas Cyber Command — DPS retains operational responsibility for its own systems.

powerStructure.name: Public Safety Commission
powerStructure.title: Governing five-member board
powerStructure.note: Governor-appointed commission that oversees DPS and approves major leadership and policy decisions — the governance layer above the Director.

news.title: Texas remains on the sidelines as digital driver's licenses expand nationwide
news.impact: Signals a likely future mobile-DL / digital-identity modernization track at DPS — an opening to position secure issuance and identity infrastructure ahead of a program.

news.title: DPS rolls out new, harder-to-counterfeit DL/ID card design
news.impact: Active investment in driver license security and issuance — adjacent to platform, data, and fraud-prevention modernization needs.

news.title: SB 1 funds DPS at ~$3.8B for FY26–27 with record border-security and new Homeland Security Division
news.impact: Confirms budget capacity and a reorganized homeland-security buying center — recalibrate where border/intelligence tech decisions sit.

news.title: Col. Freeman F. Martin assumes command as DPS Director
news.impact: New top-of-house leadership with a homeland-security and border background — reset the executive relationship map.

news.title: DPS solicits cloud-based maintenance and support for MBIS biometrics system
news.impact: Direct infrastructure, storage, and disaster-recovery opportunity around a mission-critical biometrics platform — a concrete entry point.

dellActions: Build the executive map under Director Col. Freeman F. Martin and CIO Jessica Ballew, and target the OSD service area as the IT and infrastructure buying center.
Pursue the MBIS biometrics modernization — position secure data-center, storage, high-availability, and disaster-recovery infrastructure for a mission-critical, CJIS-governed system.
Lead with edge and mobile (ruggedized field devices, mobile/in-vehicle compute) plus AI/analytics for the fusion center and border-surveillance operations funded by record border appropriations.
Confirm Dell's DIR Cooperative Contract coverage as the primary access path, and clear TX-RAMP and CJIS requirements early for any cloud or as-a-service play touching DPS data.
Watch for a future Texas digital/mobile driver license program and position identity-issuance and secure-data infrastructure ahead of the RFP.
